skill.name: github-workflow-automation
skill.description: Guides AI-assisted GitHub workflow automation for pull request review, issue triage, CI/CD validation, Git operations, and repository governance.
skill.summary: Build AI-assisted GitHub workflows for reviews, triage, CI/CD, and repository maintenance.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: Static analysis flagged many command execution, credential, network, and filesystem patterns. Review found these are mainly GitHub Actions and TypeScript examples for legitimate DevOps automation, not hidden executable behavior or confirmed exfiltration. Several examples still need user warnings because they interpolate untrusted GitHub input into shell commands and AI prompts.

medium_findings.title: Unsanitized Git Command Construction in Examples
medium_findings.description: The smart cherry-pick example builds git commands with commitHash, targetBranch, and affectedFiles inside template literals. This is a command injection risk if those values come from users, branches, tags, or workflow inputs without validation.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The command execution pattern is explicit and uses interpolated variables. The file is documentation, so the risk depends on whether users copy the example into automation.

medium_findings.title: Untrusted GitHub Comment Data Interpolated Into Shell
medium_findings.description: The mention bot extracts a public comment body through GitHub expression interpolation inside a shell command. A crafted comment could alter shell behavior or corrupt workflow outputs if copied without safe quoting and multiline output handling.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The source is a GitHub comment, which is user controlled. The example places it into a shell command and output assignment without strong escaping.

medium_findings.title: AI Automation Uses Untrusted Repository Context Without Prompt Boundaries
medium_findings.description: The review and mention bot examples send PR diffs, issue bodies, and comments directly to an AI model. Malicious repository text could influence the model response unless the workflow adds prompt injection boundaries and permission limits.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The examples clearly pass untrusted project or comment content into AI prompts. There is no evidence of malicious intent, but the missing boundaries are a practical automation risk.

low_findings.title: Credential and Network Patterns Are Expected for GitHub Automation
low_findings.description: The examples reference Anthropic and GitHub tokens and include GitHub documentation links. These are legitimate for the skill purpose, but users should keep tokens in GitHub Secrets and use minimum workflow permissions.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The token and URL references are visible in workflow examples and resource links. They support the stated DevOps use case and do not show exfiltration to an unrelated endpoint.

low_findings.title: Static Weak Crypto and C2 Flags Are False Positives
low_findings.description: The flagged words appear in the skill name, review text, CODEOWNERS examples, or general workflow labels. No evidence found of cryptographic implementation, command and control behavior, or malicious network endpoints.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Manual review found ordinary documentation context around the flagged terms. There is no executable crypto code or C2-like communication pattern in the skill file.

dangerous_patterns.title: Template Literal Shell Commands
dangerous_patterns.description: The examples show shell commands built with template literals and dynamic git values. Prefer argument arrays or strict allowlists for branch names, commit hashes, and file paths.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The code visibly interpolates variables into command strings. The pattern is dangerous when copied into real automation with untrusted values.

dangerous_patterns.title: GitHub Expression Data Inside Shell
dangerous_patterns.description: The workflow examples place GitHub event data and outputs into shell scripts. These values should be passed through environment variables and validated before use.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The pattern is visible in GitHub Actions snippets. Some uses are low risk, but comment body handling is clearly user controlled.

dangerous_patterns.title: AI Prompts From Untrusted PR and Issue Content
dangerous_patterns.description: The examples feed diffs, issue bodies, and comments into AI prompts. Production workflows need prompt injection handling and constrained repository permissions.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The data flow into AI prompts is explicit. The risk is semantic and depends on deployment permissions.

content.user_title: Automate GitHub Reviews and CI Workflows
content.value_statement: GitHub teams spend time on repeatable review, triage, and release tasks. This skill provides practical workflow patterns for AI-assisted automation in Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.
actual_capabilities: Provides GitHub Actions examples for AI pull request review comments.
Shows issue triage patterns for labels, severity, areas, and initial responses.
Outlines CI/CD workflows for smart test selection and deployment risk checks.
Includes examples for rebasing, cherry-picking, rollback, and stale branch cleanup.
Documents repository governance patterns such as CODEOWNERS and branch protection.
limitations: Examples are templates and require project-specific validation before production use.
AI provider functions such as callAI and ai.chat are placeholders, not complete integrations.
Workflow snippets need secret management, permissions review, and input hardening.
The skill does not run GitHub workflows or connect to GitHub by itself.

use_cases.title: Add AI PR Reviews
use_cases.description: Create a workflow that reviews pull request diffs and posts structured feedback for maintainers.

use_cases.title: Triage Incoming Issues
use_cases.description: Classify new issues by type, severity, and area, then apply labels and request missing reproduction details.

use_cases.title: Improve Release Safety
use_cases.description: Use change analysis, rollback patterns, and branch protection examples to support safer deployments.

prompt_templates.title: Create Basic PR Review Workflow
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner setup for AI pull request review.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to draft a GitHub Actions workflow that reviews pull request diffs with Claude. Keep permissions minimal and explain required secrets.

prompt_templates.title: Design Issue Triage Automation
prompt_templates.scenario: Intermediate setup for issue labels and responses.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to design issue triage automation for my repository. Include labels, severity rules, missing information checks, and safe default behavior.

prompt_templates.title: Plan CI Test Selection
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to map changed file paths to test suites in GitHub Actions. Include fallback behavior when the changed area is unclear.

prompt_templates.title: Harden an AI GitHub Bot
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced security review before production rollout.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to review my AI GitHub bot design for token scope, prompt injection, shell injection, and safe handling of untrusted PR content.

output_examples.input: Request to add AI review comments on pull requests.
output_examples.output: A workflow plan with trigger events, minimal permissions, changed-file collection, AI review steps, and review comment behavior.

output_examples.input: Request to automate issue triage for a growing project.
output_examples.output: A triage design with issue categories, severity levels, label mapping, response templates, and maintainer review points.

output_examples.input: Request to prepare safer release automation.
output_examples.output: A release workflow outline covering risk assessment, manual approval triggers, rollback steps, and branch protection requirements.

best_practices: Store provider keys and GitHub tokens in secrets, and grant only the permissions each job needs.
Validate branch names, commit hashes, file paths, comments, and workflow inputs before shell or git use.
Treat PR diffs, issue bodies, and comments as untrusted content when building AI prompts.
anti_patterns: Do not copy shell interpolation examples into production without quoting, validation, and safe output handling.
Do not let an AI bot approve, merge, deploy, or force push without human controls.
Do not expose full repository context or secret-derived data to an AI model when a smaller context is enough.

faq.question: Does this skill execute GitHub workflows?
faq.answer: No. It provides patterns and examples that you adapt inside your own repository.

faq.question: Which AI tools does it support?
faq.answer: The marketplace entry lists Claude, Codex, and Claude Code as supported tools.

faq.question: Do I need GitHub Actions experience?
faq.answer: Basic GitHub Actions knowledge helps because the skill uses workflow snippets and job permissions.

faq.question: Can I use it for private repositories?
faq.answer: Yes, but review token scopes, model data handling, and repository privacy requirements first.

faq.question: Are the examples production ready?
faq.answer: No. They are starting points and need validation, secrets handling, and security hardening.

faq.question: What is the main security concern?
faq.answer: The main concern is unsafe handling of untrusted comments, diffs, branch names, and commit values.
